findings.description: Bare `except Exception` catches are the dominant error handling pattern across I/O boundaries. While these log via `log.exception()`, they catch far too broadly — a KeyError, TypeError, or programming bug in extract_tags() would be silently swallowed the same way as an httpx.HTTPError. Compare with preflight.py and tags.py:get_latest_tags() which catch specific exception types (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError). The codebase is split between these two approaches with no clear rule for when to use which.

These functions catch Exception and log but never propagate. The callers have no way to know whether the operation succeeded. This is intentional (best-effort cleanup), but the same pattern is used in claims.py where failure semantics are different — claim_processing_tag returns False on failure, giving the caller a signal. The inconsistency means some error paths are observable and others are not.

The method catches httpx.HTTPStatusError for 400 status to try alternate matching_algorithm representations, but re-raises on non-400 or last candidate. This is a well-structured retry pattern, but the implicit return of None when the loop exhausts without the raise path is a type-safety gap — the method signature claims to return dict[str, Any] but could theoretically return None if logic changes.

Returns None on BadRequestError (compat exhausted) and on APIError, but the retry decorator in OpenAIChatMixin._create_completion raises on exhausted retries. Two different failure signaling strategies (None vs raise) exist in the same call chain, requiring the caller (classify_text) to handle both.

OCR worker wraps the initial get_document in _refresh_document() which returns None on failure (silently continuing). Classifier worker calls get_document directly and lets exceptions propagate to the daemon_loop's threadpool handler. Different failure strategies for the same lifecycle phase.

The module docstring is a multi-line RST-formatted docstring (lines 1-11) that restates what the module contains — information already obvious from reading the file. This is a documentation format island; no other module uses RST section headers in its docstring. The ocr/prompts.py equivalent has a clean one-liner.

Both __init__.py files contain multi-line module docstrings with a 'Modules' section that enumerates every module in the package with descriptions. This is a maintenance liability (goes stale on renames) and restates what `ls` or the imports below already show. Other modules in the codebase have clean one-line docstrings.

init_llm_semaphore() is a one-line pass-through to llm_limiter.init(). llm_semaphore() is a two-line pass-through context manager wrapping llm_limiter.acquire(). These add no policy, translation, or error handling — callers could use llm_limiter directly. This is classic LLM-generated wrapper boilerplate.

Both _claim_processing_tag methods are single-expression pass-throughs to common.claims.claim_processing_tag, adding only the keyword argument names. Similarly _finalize_with_error in both workers just forwards to the common function. Four thin delegation wrappers that add no logic.

These two methods are nearly identical — both parse comma-separated env vars with fallback to defaults. _get_model_list adds one extra check (raise if empty). The duplication suggests generated code that was not consolidated.

is_refusal and is_error_content perform the same logical operation — check if text contains any marker from a list OR contains redaction markers. They share the contains_redacted_marker helper but duplicate the any(...) pattern. This is a hallmark of generated code where similar functions exist in different modules without consolidation.

OcrProvider is an ABC with one concrete implementation (OpenAIProvider) and no evidence of extension pressure (no plugin registry, no config-driven dispatch, no second provider). The get_stats() method on the ABC returns an empty dict, making it a default-do-nothing hook. This is a speculative abstraction — the mixin-based design (OpenAIChatMixin) already provides the reuse mechanism.

LLMConcurrencyLimiter wraps a threading.Semaphore behind init()/acquire() with a module-level singleton plus two free-function wrappers. The class adds deferred initialization (init separate from __init__) and the no-op path for unlimited concurrency. A simpler approach would be a module-level function that creates and stores the semaphore directly, reducing three abstraction layers to one.

Uses getattr(settings, processing_tag_id_attr) to dynamically look up tag ID attributes by string name. This indirection exists solely to avoid two nearly identical bootstrap functions. The string-based attribute access trades type safety for DRY — a mypy/pyright checker cannot verify the attribute names are valid.

get_or_create_correspondent_id, get_or_create_document_type_id, and get_or_create_tag_ids follow the same lookup-or-create-with-retry-refresh pattern but cannot be unified because each uses a different normalizer, allow_substring flag, and create method. The triplication is justified by the different behaviors, but the refresh-on-failure pattern could be extracted.

This method is the longest in the codebase at ~50 lines and mixes multiple concerns: error-tag detection, date parsing, tag building, taxonomy resolution, pipeline tag cleanup, custom field handling, and the final API call. The sequential composition is clear, but the method does too many things to be easily testable in isolation. Contrast with how _truncate_content was extracted as a focused helper.

The segment slicing logic (lines 148-152) uses a special case for idx==0 where start=0 to capture preamble before the first page header. This means segment[0] may contain content that is NOT part of page 1 (e.g. metadata before the first --- Page 1 --- header). The asymmetry is correct but non-obvious; a comment or variable name like 'preamble_and_first_page' would clarify intent.

The three if-blocks for temperature, response_format, and max_tokens errors are structurally identical (check error type, log, inc stat, pop param, continue). This could be a data-driven loop over (detector, param_name, stat_key) tuples, reducing 30 lines to ~10. The current form is readable but repetitive.

The method builds a payload dict by checking each parameter against None individually (7 if-blocks). This is direct but mechanical. A dict comprehension filtering None values from a mapping would be more concise, though arguably less explicit about the Paperless API field name mapping.
Clean, minimal implementation of range compression. The start/prev tracking with a single append after the loop is a well-executed pattern with no wasted branches.

The polling loop decomposition into run_polling_threadpool -> _poll_once -> _process_batch is well-layered. Each function has a clear single responsibility and the was_idle flag for log suppression is a nice touch that avoids noisy output without hiding information.

Clean three-package decomposition (common, ocr, classifier) with coherent ownership. Each package maps to a domain capability. common/ provides shared infrastructure (config, paperless client, retry, daemon loop, tag lifecycle), ocr/ handles document-to-text, classifier/ handles text-to-metadata. Entry points are symmetric (daemon.py -> worker.py pattern in both). A new engineer can predict file placement.

Settings is a monolithic class serving both daemons. It carries ~30 fields, mixing OCR-specific (OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS) and classifier-specific (CLASSIFY_MAX_PAGES, CLASSIFY_TAG_LIMIT, CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID) concerns. At this project size this is acceptable and avoids premature abstraction, but it means both daemons load irrelevant configuration. The class is well-organized with grouped _load_*() methods.

ClassificationProcessor.process() is well-structured: a clear 9-step workflow documented in the docstring, with each step delegating to focused private methods (_truncate_content, _claim_processing_tag, _build_tag_names, _resolve_taxonomy_ids, _apply_classification). The try/finally pattern correctly ensures lock release. Functions are focused and single-responsibility.

PaperlessClient._get/_patch/_post methods accept *args/**kwargs, forwarding them to httpx. This loses type information and makes the API surface opaque. The methods also duplicate the pattern of setting timeout default and calling _raise_for_status_if_server_error. A single _request(method, ...) helper could reduce this repetition while making the signature explicit.

_create_with_compat handles three separate parameter-stripping paths (temperature, response_format, max_tokens) with an identical pattern: detect error -> log -> pop param -> increment stat -> continue. This is a repeated structural pattern that could be data-driven (a list of (param_name, detector_fn, stat_key) tuples), but at three entries the current approach is still readable.

get_or_create_correspondent_id, get_or_create_document_type_id, and get_or_create_tag_ids share a common pattern: lock -> match -> if not found: create -> on create failure: refresh + retry match -> raise. The first two are nearly identical except for the normalizer and allow_substring flag. This could be refactored into a generic _get_or_create_item, but at three methods with slightly different signatures it remains manageable.

Both daemon entry points follow an identical pattern: bootstrap_daemon() -> create client/provider -> define process_document closure -> run_polling_threadpool(). The handoff from daemon to worker is clean: daemon creates per-document instances of PaperlessClient and provider, passes them to the worker constructor, and ensures cleanup in a finally block. The closure-based approach avoids needing a factory class.

The _apply_classification method calls extract_tags(document) twice -- once in process() at line 86 and again inside _apply_classification at lines 268 and 280. Each call hits a different code path with a different context string, but they extract from the same document dict. This creates subtle inconsistency: the tags extracted in process() are used for error-tag checking, while _apply_classification re-extracts for its own tag manipulation. Passing the already-extracted tags would be cleaner.

Tag lifecycle functions (extract_tags, clean_pipeline_tags, finalize_document_with_error, release_processing_tag) form a coherent API surface with consistent patterns: all accept PaperlessClient + doc_id, handle errors gracefully, and log with structured context. Error propagation is predictable -- release_processing_tag and remove_stale_queue_tag swallow exceptions (best-effort), while finalize_document_with_error propagates.

OpenAIChatMixin uses the global openai module state (openai.chat.completions.create) rather than receiving a configured client instance. This means library_setup.py must configure openai at module level before any provider is used, creating a temporal coupling. The mixin also assumes self.settings exists with retry parameters -- this implicit contract is documented but enforced only at runtime.

Test structure mirrors source layout well: unit tests per module, integration tests for cross-module data flow, and E2E tests for full workflow scenarios. The test helper layer (factories.py, mocks.py) centralizes test object creation. Test count (~830) is generous for ~4K LOC. Both happy path and error paths are covered for the worker orchestrators.

Worker tests heavily patch internal functions (claim_processing_tag, release_processing_tag, truncate_content_by_pages, enrich_tags, normalize_language, needs_error_tag, update_custom_fields). This creates tight coupling to implementation: if the worker refactors how it calls these functions (e.g., inlining logic or changing import paths), tests break without any behavior change. The E2E tests partially mitigate this by exercising real function chains.

No tests exist for the daemon entry points (classifier/daemon.py, ocr/daemon.py) or the bootstrap sequence (common/bootstrap.py) as integrated units. These files wire together settings, preflight, stale lock recovery, and the polling loop -- a critical orchestration path. A test verifying that bootstrap_daemon returns None on preflight failure, or that the daemon correctly passes callbacks to run_polling_threadpool, would catch wiring errors.

Tests use no snapshot patterns and no timing-dependent assertions. Thread pool tests use PAGE_WORKERS=1 for deterministic ordering. The E2E tests use stateful mock clients that track tag mutations across calls, which is a good pattern for testing the multi-step claim-verify workflow. No flaky test patterns observed.

No unit test file exists for ocr/prompts.py. While it is a constants module, the TRANSCRIPTION_PROMPT is a critical piece of the system. More importantly, the OcrProvider.transcribe_image method's model fallback loop is only tested at the E2E level with mocks, not with targeted unit tests for edge cases like: all models refuse, only the last model succeeds, API error on first model then success on second.

OcrProvider ABC has only one concrete implementation (OpenAIProvider). There is no current evidence of extension pressure (no plugin system, no alternative backends beyond ollama which uses the same OpenAI-compatible API). The ABC adds a layer of indirection without current value. However, it does cleanly separate the interface from the implementation, and the overhead is minimal.

The OCR worker cleanly separates concerns across the pipeline: download -> convert (image_converter) -> OCR pages in parallel (provider) -> assemble text (text_assembly) -> update Paperless. Each step has a clear input/output contract. The image cleanup in the finally block ensures resources are released even on OCR failure. Data translation happens at obvious boundaries.

Clean three-package layering with no cycles. The dependency direction is strictly common <- ocr and common <- classifier, with zero cross-imports between ocr and classifier. Both domain packages import from common but never from each other. No shared mutable state or import-time side effects detected.
findings.evidence: grep for 'from classifier' in src/common/ and src/ocr/ returns empty; grep for 'from ocr' in src/common/ and src/classifier/ returns empty. All cross-package imports flow downward to common/.

findings.description: common/tags.py directly imports httpx for exception types used in error handling (httpx.HTTPError in get_latest_tags). This is a minor boundary leak: a helper module in common reaches past the PaperlessClient abstraction to reference the HTTP library's exception type directly. Ideally the PaperlessClient would either handle this internally or re-export/wrap the exception type.
findings.evidence: src/common/tags.py line 7: 'import httpx', used on line 41: 'except (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError)'

findings.description: openai SDK is imported directly in both domain provider modules (classifier/provider.py, ocr/provider.py) in addition to common/llm.py and common/preflight.py. This is acceptable since the providers are the actual consumers of the API, and common/llm.py provides a mixin rather than a complete facade. The boundary is consistent.
findings.evidence: openai imports appear in: common/llm.py, common/preflight.py, common/library_setup.py, classifier/provider.py, ocr/provider.py

findings.description: Naming conventions are highly consistent. All files use snake_case, all classes use PascalCase, all functions use snake_case. Both __init__.py files in domain packages follow the same pattern: docstring listing modules, selective re-exports, and __all__. common/__init__.py is empty (docstring only), which is a reasonable choice for the shared utilities package.
findings.evidence: Reviewed all 37 Python files. No camelCase functions or inconsistent naming found. Both classifier/__init__.py and ocr/__init__.py follow identical structural patterns.

findings.description: Both daemon entry points (ocr/daemon.py and classifier/daemon.py) follow an identical structural pattern: private _process_document helper, private _iter_docs iterator, and public main() function. This is good consistency. Minor note: the classifier daemon has a _process_document function that creates its own PaperlessClient per call, same as the OCR daemon, keeping the pattern symmetric.
findings.evidence: ocr/daemon.py defines _process_document, _iter_docs_to_ocr, main; classifier/daemon.py defines _process_document, _iter_docs_to_classify, main

findings.description: All 5 declared dependencies (httpx, openai, Pillow, pdf2image, structlog) are actively used in the codebase. No duplicate libraries for the same purpose. httpx is the sole HTTP client, openai is the sole LLM SDK, structlog is the sole logging framework. Version pinning uses compatible-release (~=) which is good practice.
findings.evidence: pyproject.toml declares 5 deps. grep confirms all are imported: httpx (4 files), openai (5 files), Pillow (4 files), pdf2image (1 file), structlog (19 files).

findings.description: pdf2image is used in exactly one file (ocr/image_converter.py) for PDF-to-image conversion. This is a relatively heavy dependency that requires poppler system binaries. However, PDF conversion is a core requirement for an OCR daemon, so this is justified usage rather than a light-use concern.
findings.evidence: src/ocr/image_converter.py:8 'from pdf2image import convert_from_bytes' is the only usage

findings.description: Pillow is imported in common/library_setup.py purely for configuration (setting MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS), but the actual image processing happens only in ocr/. This means common/ has a runtime dependency on Pillow even though the classifier daemon technically never processes images. This is a minor coupling issue.
findings.evidence: src/common/library_setup.py:10 'from PIL import Image' — used to set Image.MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS

findings.description: The three-package layout (common, ocr, classifier) under src/ is clean and well-organized. Package sizes are proportional to responsibility: common (17 files, shared infrastructure), classifier (13 files, classification domain), ocr (7 files, OCR domain). The src/ layout with setuptools find follows Python best practice.
findings.evidence: File counts: common/ has 17 .py files (1676 lines), classifier/ has 13 .py files (1509 lines), ocr/ has 7 .py files (748 lines). Total 3933 lines.

findings.description: common/ has 17 files which is on the higher side, but each file has a clear, focused responsibility (config, paperless client, retry, shutdown, tags, etc.). The module could potentially benefit from sub-grouping (e.g., lifecycle/ for bootstrap+shutdown+preflight, or api/ for paperless+retry), but at this scale the flat layout remains navigable.
findings.evidence: common/ files: bootstrap.py, claims.py, concurrency.py, config.py, daemon_loop.py, document_iter.py, library_setup.py, llm.py, logging_config.py, paperless.py, preflight.py, retry.py, shutdown.py, stale_lock.py, tags.py, utils.py

findings.description: The classifier package has well-separated concerns with dedicated modules for constants, normalizers, content_prep, quality_gates, tag_filters, metadata, taxonomy, result, prompts, provider, worker, and daemon. This is good domain-driven decomposition, though the number of small modules (normalizers at 58 lines, quality_gates at 29 lines, constants at 53 lines) suggests they could potentially be consolidated.
findings.evidence: classifier/ has 13 files. Several are quite small: quality_gates.py (29 lines), constants.py (53 lines), normalizers.py (58 lines)
